questions.question:
Ram and Shyam shared a secret and promised to each other that it would remain between them. Ram expressed himself in one of the following ways as given in the choices below. Identify the correct way as per standard English.

A. It would remain between you and me.
B. It would remain between I and you.
C. It would remain between you and I.
D. It would remain with me.
questions.answer: A. It would remain between you and me.
questions.to_find: The grammatically correct sentence describing the secret remaining between two people

understand.plain: After a preposition like 'between', English requires object pronouns (me, him, her, us, them), not subject pronouns (I, he, she, we, they). 'Between you and me' uses both object pronouns correctly. Options B and C incorrectly use 'I' after 'between', which violates this rule. Option D changes the meaning entirely.

keywords.explain: A pronoun that serves as the object of a verb or preposition; 'me', 'him', 'her', 'us', 'them' are object pronouns
A word showing the relationship between a noun/pronoun and other words in a sentence; it is always followed by an object (object pronoun)
keywords.example: 'Between' is a preposition, so it takes 'me', not 'I'

keywords.explain: A pronoun used as the subject of a verb; 'I', 'he', 'she', 'we', 'they' are subject pronouns and cannot follow a preposition
keywords.example: I went to the store. (subject of 'went')

understand.visual_alt: A preposition followed by an object pronoun, illustrating 'between you and me'

given.aim: Identify the sentence that correctly uses standard English grammar after the preposition 'between'

terms.meaning: A preposition indicating a relationship involving two or more parties; must be followed by object pronouns
terms.example: The secret remains between you and me.
terms.connects: Governs the choice of pronoun form (object, not subject)

terms.meaning: 'Me' is an object pronoun used after prepositions; 'I' is a subject pronoun used as the sentence subject
terms.example: He called me (object); I called him (subject)

terms.meaning: The prescriptive grammar rule requiring object case after prepositions
terms.example: between us, for him, with her — all object forms after prepositions
terms.connects: Eliminates options B and C which use 'I' after 'between'

given.plan: Identify the preposition 'between' in the sentence, then verify each option uses the correct object pronoun form after the preposition. Object pronouns (me, you, him, us) must follow prepositions, not subject pronouns (I).

steps.title: Identify the preposition and required case
steps.apply: The word 'between' is a preposition. English grammar requires that pronouns following prepositions be in the object case. The object form of 'I' is 'me'.
steps.note: This is a fundamental pronoun case rule: prepositions always govern the objective case.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A: 'between you and me' — both 'you' and 'me' are object pronouns. CORRECT. B: 'between I and you' — 'I' is a subject pronoun after a preposition. INCORRECT. C: 'between you and I' — 'I' is a subject pronoun after a preposition. INCORRECT. D: 'It would remain with me' — changes meaning; does not convey the shared secret. INCORRECT.
steps.note: Option C is a common hypercorrection error (people mistakenly believe 'I' sounds more formal), but it violates grammar.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Option A correctly uses 'between you and me' where both pronouns are in the objective case as required after the preposition 'between'.
steps.note: The answer is A.

solution.result: Option A: 'It would remain between you and me.' is the correct standard English usage.

questions.formula_note: This question tests pronoun case (object vs subject pronoun after a preposition) — a grammar rule, not a mathematical formula.

questions.question:
Which of the following is CORRECT with respect to grammar and usage?

Mount Everest is ________.

A. The highest peak in the world
B. Highest peak in the world
C. One of highest peak in the world
D. One of the highest peak in the world
questions.answer: A. The highest peak in the world
questions.to_find: The grammatically correct completion describing Mount Everest using proper article and superlative usage

understand.plain: When a noun is uniquely identified by a superlative (the one and only highest peak), the definite article 'the' is mandatory. Mount Everest holds the unique distinction of being THE highest peak, so it requires 'the highest peak'. 'One of the highest peaks' would be grammatical for non-unique entities, but Everest IS uniquely the highest. Options B and C omit 'the', and option D uses singular 'peak' after 'one of the', which requires a plural noun.

keywords.explain: Superlatives (highest, tallest, best) always require 'the' before them because they identify a unique entity among a group
keywords.example: She is the tallest student in class (not 'tallest student')

keywords.explain: The phrase 'one of the' must be followed by a plural noun because it refers to one member of a group
keywords.example: She is one of the best students (not 'one of the best student')

keywords.explain: Used before nouns that are uniquely identified or already known to the listener/reader; mandatory before superlatives
keywords.example: The sun, the highest mountain, the best answer

understand.visual_alt: A comparison showing Mount Everest as uniquely the single highest peak, illustrated by article usage

given.aim: Identify the correct article and number (singular/plural) usage in a sentence about Mount Everest's superlative status

terms.meaning: The rule that 'the' must precede a superlative adjective when identifying a unique entity
terms.example: He is the smartest person in the room.
terms.connects: Eliminates option B which omits 'the' before 'highest'

terms.meaning: The idiomatic phrase 'one of the' must be followed by a plural noun
terms.example: She is one of the fastest runners.
terms.connects: Eliminates option D which incorrectly uses singular 'peak' after 'one of the'

terms.meaning: Mount Everest is THE highest peak (uniquely), not merely one of many highest peaks; this grammatical choice reflects factual uniqueness
terms.example: Everest is THE highest — unique; K2 is one of the highest — among several
terms.connects: Confirms option A as the correct unique superlative form

given.plan: Check each option for: (1) correct use of 'the' before a superlative, (2) subject-verb agreement, and (3) singular/plural noun agreement after 'one of the'. A unique superlative requires 'the'; 'one of the' requires plural.

steps.title: Rule: superlative always takes 'the'
steps.apply: Superlative adjectives ('highest', 'tallest', 'best') require the definite article 'the' in front. Option B ('Highest peak in the world') omits 'the' — grammatically incorrect.
steps.note: This rule is absolute in standard English for superlatives.

steps.title: Rule: 'one of the' requires plural noun
steps.apply: Options C and D use the phrase 'one of ... peak'. Option C: 'One of highest peak' — missing 'the' AND uses singular 'peak'. Option D: 'One of the highest peak' — has 'the' but uses singular 'peak'; should be 'peaks'. Both are incorrect.
steps.note: After 'one of the', the noun must be plural since you are choosing one from a group of several.

steps.title: Verify Option A
steps.apply: Option A: 'The highest peak in the world' — uses 'the' before the superlative 'highest', uses singular noun 'peak' correctly (it is the unique one). This is grammatically perfect.
steps.note: Mount Everest is uniquely the highest, so the superlative with 'the' (not 'one of the highest') is the most accurate statement.

solution.result: Option A: 'The highest peak in the world' is grammatically correct — 'the' is mandatory before the superlative 'highest'.

questions.formula_note: This question tests the definite article rule before superlatives and the plural noun requirement after 'one of the' — standard English grammar rules.

questions.question:
"Going by the _____ that many hands make light work, the school _____ involved all the students in the task."

The words that best fill the blanks in the above sentence are:

A. principle, principal
B. principal, principle
C. principle, principle
D. principal, principal
questions.to_find: The correct pair of homophones (principle/principal) to fill the two blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: The two words 'principle' and 'principal' are homophones (they sound alike) but have completely different meanings. 'Principle' is a noun meaning a fundamental rule or belief. 'Principal' can be a noun meaning the head of a school or an adjective meaning main/chief. In the sentence, the first blank refers to the proverb (a fundamental belief/rule), so 'principle' is needed. The second blank refers to the school's head administrator, so 'principal' is needed.

keywords.explain: A noun referring to a fundamental rule, belief, or moral standard; often used in phrases like 'as a matter of principle' or 'the principle of fairness'
keywords.example: The principle that honesty is the best policy guides his decisions.

keywords.explain: As a noun, the head of a school or organization; as an adjective, meaning main or chief
keywords.example: The school principal announced a new policy.

keywords.explain: Words that sound the same but have different spellings and meanings

understand.visual_alt: Two blank slots in the sentence, the first pointing to a proverb (principle) and the second to the school head (principal)

given.aim: Select the correct homophone pair for the two blanks: a proverb/rule word and a school-head word

terms.meaning: The phrase 'going by the ___' is followed by a proverb ('many hands make light work'), indicating a rule or guiding belief
terms.example: Going by the principle that practice makes perfect, she rehearsed daily.

terms.meaning: 'The school ___' refers to the head administrator of the school — the principal
terms.example: The school principal organized the sports day event.
terms.connects: The second blank needs 'principal' (the head of the school)

terms.meaning: A common memory aid: the school PRINCIPAL is your PAL; a PRINCIPLE is a rule ending in -ple like 'example'
terms.example: PrincipAL = the person; principLE = the rule
terms.connects: Helps distinguish the two words to correctly pair them

given.plan: Identify what each blank means in context: blank 1 follows 'going by the' before a proverb (needs 'principle' = rule), blank 2 follows 'the school' as a person/head (needs 'principal' = head administrator). Match definitions to select option A.

steps.title: Analyze the first blank
steps.apply: The sentence reads: 'Going by the _____ that many hands make light work'. The phrase 'going by the ___ that [proverb]' introduces a guiding rule or belief. The word that means a fundamental rule or guiding belief is 'principle'. So the first blank = 'principle'.
steps.note: The that-clause 'that many hands make light work' is the content of the rule, confirming 'principle'.

steps.title: Analyze the second blank
steps.apply: The sentence reads: 'the school _____ involved all the students in the task'. Here 'the school _____' refers to a person — the head of the school who took an action (involved all students). The word for the head of a school is 'principal'. So the second blank = 'principal'.
steps.note: 'Principal' here is a noun meaning the head administrator of the school.

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.apply: First blank = 'principle', second blank = 'principal'. This matches option A: (principle, principal). Options B, C, D get at least one blank wrong.
steps.note: Answer is A.

solution.result: A. (principle, principal) — 'principle' fills the first blank (a guiding rule/proverb) and 'principal' fills the second blank (head of the school).

questions.formula_note: This question tests homophone disambiguation — choosing between 'principle' (a rule) and 'principal' (head of school/main) based on sentence context.

questions.question:
The lecture was attended by quite _______ students, so the hall was not very _______.

A. a few, quite
B. few, quiet
C. a few, quiet
D. few, quite
questions.to_find: The correct pair of words to fill the blanks, distinguishing 'few' from 'a few' and 'quite' from 'quiet'

understand.plain: The sentence has two distinct issues: (1) 'few' vs 'a few': 'few' means hardly any (negative tone), while 'a few' means some (positive tone). Since the hall was present and a lecture took place, 'a few' (some students attended) makes logical sense. (2) 'quite' vs 'quiet': 'quite' is an adverb of degree, while 'quiet' is an adjective meaning low noise. The hall not being 'quiet' (silent) makes contextual sense — a few students created some noise.

keywords.explain: Means 'some' or 'a small number' with a positive or neutral tone — enough to be worth mentioning
keywords.example: A few students attended the optional lecture (some showed up).

keywords.explain: Means 'hardly any' with a negative or emphatic tone — suggesting an insufficient or disappointing number
keywords.example: Few students passed the exam (disappointingly low numbers).

keywords.explain: An adjective meaning making little or no noise; calm and silent
keywords.example: The library was quiet because everyone was reading.

understand.visual_alt: A hall with some students attending a lecture — not packed but not empty, and not completely silent

given.aim: Choose the correct pair from 'few/a few' and 'quite/quiet' to complete a logically and grammatically consistent sentence

terms.meaning: The lecture had some students attend (enough to not be empty, but still not many); positive count word 'a few' is required
terms.example: A few students came even though it was optional.
terms.connects: First blank = 'a few' (some attended, positive connotation)

terms.term: second blank: state of the hall
terms.meaning: Because a few students attended, the hall was not very quiet (silent); 'quiet' is the adjective needed here
terms.example: The hall was not quiet because the students were chatting.
terms.connects: Second blank = 'quiet' (adjective for noise level, not the adverb 'quite')

terms.meaning: The second clause provides a reason linked to the first: a few students = hall not very quiet makes logical sense
terms.example: A few people were in the room, so it was not very quiet.
terms.connects: Confirms option C: a few + quiet

given.plan: For the first blank, determine if the tone is positive (a few = some) or negative (few = hardly any); the context of a lecture being attended points to 'a few'. For the second blank, identify whether an adverb (quite) or adjective (quiet) is needed after 'very'; 'very quiet' is an adjective phrase, so 'quiet' fits.

steps.title: Choose between 'few' and 'a few'
steps.apply: 'A few' = some (positive); 'few' = hardly any (negative). The lecture was attended, implying some people came. 'A few students' means some students attended — a positive, neutral count. 'Few students' would imply attendance was disappointingly low. The sentence states the hall was 'not very quiet', implying some noise from some people. So 'a few' is more appropriate for the first blank.
steps.note: 'A few' has an optimistic/neutral connotation while 'few' has a pessimistic/scarce connotation.

steps.title: Choose between 'quite' and 'quiet'
steps.apply: The second blank follows 'not very ___'. This requires an adjective to describe the state of the hall. 'Quiet' (adjective, meaning silent/calm) correctly fills this — 'the hall was not very quiet' means it had some noise. 'Quite' is an adverb of degree and cannot follow 'very' in this context to describe the hall.
steps.note: 'Very quiet' is a standard adjective phrase; 'very quite' is grammatically wrong.

steps.title: Confirm option C
steps.apply: First blank = 'a few', second blank = 'quiet'. Full sentence: 'The lecture was attended by quite a few students, so the hall was not very quiet.' This is grammatically correct and logically coherent. This matches option C.
steps.note: The answer is C.

solution.result: C. (a few, quiet) — 'a few' correctly denotes some students (positive tone), and 'quiet' correctly describes the noise level of the hall as an adjective.

questions.formula_note: This question tests two lexical distinctions: 'few' vs 'a few' (negative vs positive quantity) and 'quite' (adverb of degree) vs 'quiet' (adjective for silence).

questions.question:
Choose the grammatically INCORRECT sentence:

A. They gave us the money back less the service charges of Three Hundred rupees.
B. This country's expenditure is not less than that of Bangladesh.
C. The committee initially asked for a funding of Fifty Lakh rupees, but later settled for a lesser sum.
D. This country's expenditure on education is very less.
questions.answer: D. This country's expenditure on education is very less.
questions.to_find: The grammatically incorrect sentence among the four options, focusing on misuse of 'less', 'lesser', and 'fewer'

understand.plain: In standard English, 'less' is a comparative adjective used with uncountable or abstract quantities (less water, less money). However, 'less' should not follow 'very' — one says 'very little' not 'very less'. Option D uses 'very less' which is non-standard (an Indian English error). Options A, B, and C all use 'less' or 'lesser' correctly in standard constructions. Option D should say 'very little' or simply 'low'.

keywords.explain: A non-standard Indian English construction; in standard English, 'less' is already comparative and is not intensified by 'very'. Correct forms are 'very little' or 'much less'.
keywords.example: Incorrect: 'very less time'; Correct: 'very little time' or 'much less time'

keywords.explain: A comparative adjective meaning a smaller amount; used correctly in comparisons like 'less than' or in the sense of 'minus' (less service charges)
keywords.example: She spent less money this month than last month.

keywords.explain: A formal comparative adjective meaning smaller in size, importance, or degree; used correctly in phrases like 'lesser sum', 'the lesser evil'
keywords.example: He chose the lesser of two evils; the committee settled for a lesser sum.

understand.visual_alt: Four sentences highlighted, with option D marked as incorrect for using 'very less' instead of 'very little'

given.aim: Identify which sentence violates standard English grammar by misusing a comparative or degree word

terms.term: option A: less the service charges
terms.meaning: 'Less' used as a preposition meaning 'minus' or 'deducting'; this is standard English usage in financial contexts
terms.example: The total was one thousand less the discount.

terms.meaning: Standard comparative construction 'not less than' = at least as much as
terms.example: His salary is not less than mine.

terms.meaning: 'Lesser' is a formal adjective correctly used here to mean a smaller amount
terms.example: They accepted a lesser amount than originally requested.

given.plan: Evaluate each option for correct usage of degree words and comparatives. Look for the non-standard construction 'very less' which violates English grammar — 'very' cannot modify 'less'; the correct phrase is 'very little'.

steps.title: Check option A
steps.apply: Option A: 'gave us the money back less the service charges' — 'less' here functions as a preposition meaning 'minus/deducting'. This is standard English. CORRECT.
steps.note: 'Less' as a preposition meaning 'minus' is valid in formal English.

steps.title: Check option B
steps.apply: Option B: 'not less than that of Bangladesh' — a standard comparative structure using 'less than'. CORRECT.
steps.note: Comparing one country's expenditure to another using 'not less than' is grammatically sound.

steps.title: Check option C
steps.apply: Option C: 'settled for a lesser sum' — 'lesser' is a formal comparative adjective correctly modifying 'sum'. CORRECT.
steps.note: 'Lesser' in formal English means of a lower degree or smaller size — perfectly valid here.

steps.title: Check option D
steps.apply: Option D: 'expenditure on education is very less' — 'very less' is non-standard English. 'Less' is itself a comparative form and should not be intensified by 'very'. The correct phrase is 'very little' or 'much less'. This sentence is grammatically INCORRECT.
steps.note: In standard English: 'The expenditure is very little' or 'The expenditure is much less (than expected)'.

solution.result: D. 'This country's expenditure on education is very less' is grammatically incorrect — 'very less' is non-standard; correct usage is 'very little'.

questions.formula_note: This question tests the misuse of degree adverbs with comparative adjectives — 'very less' is a common Indian English error; standard English uses 'very little' for small uncountable quantities.

questions.question:
Choose the grammatically INCORRECT sentence:

A. He is of Asian origin.
B. They belonged to Africa.
C. She is an European.
D. They migrated from India to Australia.
questions.to_find: The grammatically incorrect sentence, specifically identifying the wrong article usage before a word starting with a consonant sound

understand.plain: The article used before a word depends on its initial SOUND, not its initial letter. 'European' begins with the letter 'E' but is pronounced 'YOO-ro-pee-an' — starting with a consonant sound 'Y' (like 'you'). Therefore it takes the indefinite article 'a', not 'an'. Option C incorrectly uses 'an European' when it should be 'a European'. The rule: use 'an' before vowel sounds (an apple, an hour) and 'a' before consonant sounds (a university, a European).

keywords.explain: The choice between 'a' and 'an' depends on the pronunciation of the following word, not its spelling
keywords.example: 'An hour' (silent h, starts with vowel sound); 'a university' (starts with consonant 'y' sound)

keywords.explain: Pronounced 'YOO-ro-pee-an'; the initial sound is the consonant 'Y', so it takes the article 'a', not 'an'
keywords.example: He is a European citizen. (not 'an European')

keywords.explain: 'An' before vowel sounds (a, e, i, o, u sounds); 'a' before consonant sounds; the initial letter may be a vowel but if it sounds like a consonant, use 'a'
keywords.example: a European (Y sound), a uniform (Y sound), an umbrella (U sound), an elephant (E sound)

understand.visual_alt: Four sentences with option C highlighted showing incorrect 'an European' versus the correct 'a European'

given.aim: Identify which sentence uses the wrong indefinite article ('an' vs 'a') by checking the initial sound of the noun

terms.meaning: No article issue; 'of Asian origin' uses no indefinite article before 'Asian' in this prepositional phrase
terms.example: He is of Asian origin — grammatically correct.

terms.meaning: No article used; 'Africa' is a proper noun used without an article — correct standard English
terms.example: They belonged to Africa — grammatically correct.

terms.meaning: 'European' starts with the consonant Y-sound, so 'a' is correct, not 'an'; using 'an' violates the sound-based article rule
terms.example: She is a European. (correct) / She is an European. (incorrect)
terms.connects: Grammatically incorrect — this is the answer

given.plan: Test each sentence for grammatical errors with special attention to article usage. For option C, check the pronunciation of 'European': it starts with the 'Y' consonant sound, requiring 'a' not 'an'. All other options are correct.

steps.title: Check options A and B
steps.apply: A: 'He is of Asian origin' — grammatically correct; 'Asian' is an adjective here with no article issue. B: 'They belonged to Africa' — correct; proper nouns for continents/countries don't take articles. Both CORRECT.
steps.note: Options A and B have no grammatical errors.

steps.title: Examine option C for article usage
steps.formula_raw: a + consonant sound word | an + vowel sound word
steps.apply: Option C: 'She is an European.' The word 'European' starts with the letter E, but its pronunciation is 'YOO-ro-pee-an' — beginning with the consonant sound 'Y'. The article rule requires 'a' before consonant sounds. So the correct form is 'She is a European', not 'an European'. This is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Similar examples: 'a uniform', 'a university', 'a one-way street' — all start with consonant sounds despite vowel letters.

steps.title: Verify option D
steps.apply: Option D: 'They migrated from India to Australia' — grammatically correct; uses proper prepositions 'from' and 'to' correctly, proper nouns without articles. CORRECT.
steps.note: D is fully grammatical.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: Only option C has a grammatical error ('an European' should be 'a European'). The answer is C.
steps.note: Answer is C.

solution.result: C. 'She is an European' is grammatically incorrect — 'European' begins with a consonant Y-sound, so it requires the article 'a', not 'an'.

questions.formula_note: This question tests the sound-based rule for choosing 'a' vs 'an': use 'a' before consonant sounds and 'an' before vowel sounds, regardless of the initial letter spelling.
